title: A signed-direction invariant for triangle tilings, and the exclusion of primes congruent to 3 modulo 4
description: Within the 2pi/3 branch of Laczkovich's classification of triangle tilings, a translation-invariant signed-direction functional proves that no prime number of 2pi/3 tiles tiles an isosceles triangle (for every prime; previously only a finite lower bound was known, and Zhang's constructions had suggested that no prime occurs). With a self-contained reduction of the scalene shapes and machine-checked replacements for four of the five 3-alpha-2-beta targets, this excludes primes N congruent to 3 modulo 4 as numbers of congruent triangles into which a triangle can be cut, with one explicit exception: the base-beta candidates N = 3f^2 - e^2 (gcd(e,f)=1, 1<=e<f), an infinite family (11, 23, 47, 59, 71, 83, 107, ...), all congruent to 3 modulo 4, for which the only published exclusion (Beeson's Theorem 14) rests on a divisibility g|M that is shown here to be false. These candidates satisfy every sound necessary condition and are settled individually by an exact corner-anchored search; those up to N=110 that have been searched (11, 23, 47, 71, 107) are excluded, and 59, 83 remain under search. In particular no triangle can be cut into 19 congruent triangles (19 is not of the exceptional form). The paper determines the admissible spectrum of each sporadic 2pi/3 branch, settles the contiguous initial segment of the spectrum, and exhibits realizations: a triangle can be cut into 28 and into 44 congruent triangles, the latter being the isosceles (16,16,22) tiled by 44 copies of the (2,3,4) tile - the smallest known tiling in an incommensurable branch - each certified by a zero-axiom Lean kernel computation. The equilateral square criteria are reduced to elementary divisor conditions, and membership in the tile-count set is shown to be decidable. The arithmetic and combinatorial core is machine-checked in Lean 4 with Mathlib (axiom-clean, no sorry); the geometric lemmas rest on the written proofs, there being no theory of triangle dissections in Mathlib. The base-beta thick regime is documented as an open problem with a partial forcing analysis. Developed with AI assistance (Anthropic Claude); presented for peer review.
